content.text: Relational databases have been the backbone of application state management for decades, and agentic AI systems are no different. When an autonomous agent orchestrates tool calls, manages multi-turn conversations, or executes actions with financial consequences, the underlying data layer must guarantee consistency, auditability, and the ability to recover from partial failures. SQL databases — PostgreSQL in particular — provide exactly these guarantees through ACID transactions, foreign-key constraints, and a rich querying language that makes ad-hoc reporting and real-time dashboards straightforward.
What it is

A SQL (Structured Query Language) database stores data in a fixed schema of tables, where each table holds rows with typed columns. Tables are linked through foreign keys — references from one row to the primary key of another — forming a relational model that can express hierarchical and many-to-many relationships while preventing orphaned data. Queries join tables on those keys, aggregate rows with GROUP BY, and filter with WHERE clauses — all expressed in declarative SQL that the database engine optimises internally.

The defining property of relational databases is ACID: Atomicity (a transaction either fully commits or fully rolls back), Consistency (constraints are never violated), Isolation (concurrent transactions do not corrupt each other's view), and Durability (a committed transaction survives a server crash). For agent platforms, ACID means that a run status update and its billing record either both land in the database or neither does — no half-committed state that leaves accounting in the dark.

PostgreSQL is the de-facto choice for new agent backends: it is open-source, extensible with pgvector and PostGIS, ships native JSONB for semi-structured tool arguments, and has a rich ecosystem of ORMs, migration tools, and managed cloud offerings (AWS RDS, Supabase, Neon, Google Cloud SQL). MySQL and SQLite are also valid for smaller workloads.
Why it matters

Agentic systems accumulate state that must outlive the in-memory lifetime of any single process. A run that calls a payment API, modifies a file, and sends a Slack message needs an immutable audit trail: which tool was called, with what arguments, and whether it succeeded. Without a durable, queryable store that record exists only in ephemeral logs, making debugging, compliance reporting, and cost attribution nearly impossible.

SQL databases solve several concrete problems simultaneously. First, idempotency: a webhook dispatcher can use a UNIQUE constraint on an event_id column to prevent double delivery even if a worker retries. Second, consistency: updating a run's status to 'completed' and crediting the user's token quota happens in one transaction — the application never observes a completed run with an unchanged quota. Third, relational reporting: a single JOIN across sessions, runs, and tool_events can answer 'which users triggered the most expensive tool calls this week?' without any application-level aggregation code.

The alternative — deferring all persistence to Redis or a document store — trades correctness for speed. It works for ephemeral caching but collapses under the requirements of financial transactions, human-in-the-loop approval records, and regulatory audits where an external auditor needs to trace exactly what the agent did and when.
Benefits
ACID transactions —
atomic updates across multiple tables prevent partial state corruption under concurrent agent workers.
Relational joins —
one query spans sessions → runs → tool_events, enabling analytics without ETL.
Foreign-key constraints —
database-enforced referential integrity prevents orphaned audit rows even when application code has bugs.
JSONB columns (PostgreSQL) —
semi-structured tool arguments are stored and indexed without a separate document database.
SQL expressiveness —
aggregations, window functions, and CTEs handle complex reporting queries that would require multiple application-level passes.
Mature ecosystem —
Alembic/Flyway for migrations, asyncpg/SQLAlchemy for async access, pgAdmin and DBeaver for inspection, and cloud-managed offerings for zero-ops operation.
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) —
WAL-based backups let you restore the database to any second in the past, critical for compliance.
Trade-offs
Schema rigidity —
every structural change (new column, renamed table) requires a migration script deployed in coordination with code changes.
Write throughput ceiling —
a single primary instance typically saturates around 10k–50k writes/second; high-frequency event streams may need a write-ahead queue (Kafka, SQS) as a buffer.
Connection overhead —
each PostgreSQL connection spawns a backend process; async agent workers can exhaust the connection pool without PgBouncer in front.
Not the right store for vectors alone —
semantic search over embeddings is possible via pgvector but a dedicated vector database (Qdrant, Weaviate) outperforms it at large scale.
Operational complexity —
backups, replication lag monitoring, vacuum configuration, and index bloat management are ongoing concerns absent from serverless alternatives.
Horizontal write scaling requires sharding —
unlike NoSQL databases, SQL primaries do not shard writes across nodes without significant architectural changes (Citus, Vitess).
How it works
The relational model

Every table has a primary key — a column or tuple of columns that uniquely identifies each row. Foreign keys reference primary keys in other tables, expressing relationships. The database enforces these constraints on every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE, so a tool_events row with a nonexistent run_id is rejected at the database layer before application code ever sees it.
Indexes are B-tree structures (by default) maintained alongside the data. A query filtering on
WHERE run_id = $1

uses the index to find matching rows in O(log n) rather than scanning the whole table. Without an index on foreign keys, every lookup is a sequential scan — fine at hundreds of rows, catastrophic at millions.
ACID mechanics

PostgreSQL implements isolation through Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC). Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot of the database as of its start time. Writers create new row versions rather than overwriting in place; old versions are cleaned up by the autovacuum process. This means readers never block writers and writers never block readers — crucial for an agent API serving high read traffic from a dashboard while workers are writing run results concurrently.

The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) is the durability mechanism. Before any data page is modified on disk, the change is recorded in the WAL. On crash, PostgreSQL replays the WAL from the last checkpoint. Streaming replication works by shipping WAL records to one or more standby servers in real time, providing both high availability and read scaling.
Transactions in application code

A transaction groups multiple SQL statements into an atomic unit. In Python's asyncpg, a transaction is managed with an async context manager. If any statement raises an exception the transaction rolls back; if the block exits cleanly it commits. Savepoints allow partial rollbacks inside a larger transaction, useful when one tool event fails but the run should continue.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the typical data layer architecture for a production agent service. The SQL database is the authoritative source of truth for all durable state. Redis provides a speed layer for ephemeral queues and caches. Object storage holds large payloads (LLM traces, uploaded files) while the SQL database stores only a reference URL. PgBouncer pools connections between the many async worker processes and the PostgreSQL primary.

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram: Agent service → PgBouncer → PostgreSQL primary with sessions, runs, tool_events, users, webhooks tables; read replica for analytics; Redis for queues; S3 for bulk storage

content.text: The key design principle is separation of concerns by data temperature and size. SQL handles small, structured, relational records that need transactional integrity. Redis handles fast ephemeral state (active run counters, distributed locks, pub/sub for live updates). S3 handles bulk objects whose size would bloat the SQL row store and inflate backup costs. All three tiers are used together; none is a replacement for the others.
When to use it
Use SQL when
The data has relationships and referential integrity matters (sessions → runs → tool_events).
You need ACID transactions (billing debit + run completion must be atomic).
Ad-hoc relational queries and aggregations are required for dashboards or compliance reports.
You need idempotency guarantees via UNIQUE constraints on natural keys (webhook event_id).
The data is structured and changes infrequently in shape (or when a migration discipline is acceptable).
Long-term auditability and point-in-time recovery are non-negotiable.
Do not use SQL as the primary store when
The payload is too large for a row (LLM message traces, uploaded PDFs) — use object storage instead.
You need sub-millisecond reads for hot paths — cache in Redis and back-fill from SQL.
The schema is unknown at design time and changes with every API version — a document store may be a better primary.
Write volume exceeds what a single primary can sustain and you cannot tolerate sharding complexity — consider a stream (Kafka) as the ingestion layer with SQL as the consumer sink.
Semantic similarity search is the dominant query pattern — add pgvector or a dedicated vector DB.
How to implement
Schema design

Start with the canonical tables that every agent platform needs, then extend per product requirements. Use UUIDs as primary keys for global uniqueness across shards and environments. TIMESTAMPTZ (timezone-aware timestamp) for all time columns — never TIMESTAMP without timezone. JSONB for tool arguments and metadata where the shape is dynamic.
Async access from Python

In async frameworks (FastAPI, Starlette), use an async driver. SQLAlchemy 2 async with asyncpg is the most common choice: it provides an ORM, raw SQL execution, and connection pooling. Never use synchronous psycopg2 in an async route without offloading to a thread pool — it blocks the event loop.

# requirements: sqlalchemy[asyncio] asyncpg alembic
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker, AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy import text
import os

DATABASE_URL = os.environ["DATABASE_URL"]  # postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@host/db

engine = create_async_engine(
    DATABASE_URL,
    pool_size=10,
    max_overflow=20,
    pool_pre_ping=True,   # detect stale connections
    echo=False,
)

AsyncSessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(engine, expire_on_commit=False)

async def get_session():
    """FastAPI dependency that yields a DB session."""
    async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
        yield session

# In a route:
async def create_run(session_id: str, model: str, db: AsyncSession):
    result = await db.execute(
        text("""
            INSERT INTO runs (session_id, status, model)
            VALUES (:session_id, 'queued', :model)
            RETURNING id
        """),
        {"session_id": session_id, "model": model},
    )
    run_id = result.scalar_one()
    await db.commit()
    return str(run_id)
Examples
Example 1 — Transactional run completion

The following Python function finalises a run and records its token usage atomically. If the billing update fails the run status stays as 'running', preventing a completed run with missing billing data.

import asyncpg
import os

async def complete_run(
    pool: asyncpg.Pool,
    run_id: str,
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    user_id: str,
) -> None:
    """
    Mark run completed and debit token quota in one transaction.
    Raises asyncpg.PostgresError on failure; caller should catch and retry.
    """
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        async with conn.transaction():
            await conn.execute(
                """
                UPDATE runs
                   SET status = 'completed',
                       finished_at = now(),
                       input_tokens  = $2,
                       output_tokens = $3
                 WHERE id = $1 AND status = 'running'
                """,
                run_id, input_tokens, output_tokens,
            )
            # Debit usage quota atomically with run completion
            await conn.execute(
                """
                INSERT INTO usage_events (user_id, run_id, tokens)
                VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
                """,
                user_id, run_id, input_tokens + output_tokens,
            )


# Bootstrap: create pool with PgBouncer-compatible settings
async def create_pool() -> asyncpg.Pool:
    return await asyncpg.create_pool(
        os.environ["DATABASE_URL"],
        min_size=2,
        max_size=10,
        command_timeout=30,
        statement_cache_size=0,  # required when behind PgBouncer in transaction mode
    )
Example 2 — Dashboard query with window functions

Window functions let you compute rolling statistics without a subquery per row. The query below returns each tool's success rate over the last 24 hours alongside the overall average — useful for an agent operations dashboard.

-- Tool success rate and rank over last 24 h (PostgreSQL)
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT
    tool_name,
    count(*)                                      AS total_calls,
    count(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'success')    AS successes,
    avg(duration_ms)                              AS avg_ms
  FROM tool_events
  WHERE created_at > now() - INTERVAL '24 hours'
  GROUP BY tool_name
)
SELECT
  tool_name,
  total_calls,
  successes,
  round(successes::numeric / NULLIF(total_calls,0) * 100, 1) AS success_pct,
  round(avg_ms)                                              AS avg_duration_ms,
  rank() OVER (ORDER BY total_calls DESC)                    AS call_rank
FROM stats
ORDER BY total_calls DESC;
Example 3 — JSONB querying for tool arguments

-- Find all tool calls that targeted a specific API endpoint (stored in args_json)
SELECT
  te.id,
  te.run_id,
  te.created_at,
  te.args_json->>'url'        AS target_url,
  te.args_json->>'method'     AS http_method
FROM tool_events te
WHERE te.tool_name = 'http_request'
  AND te.args_json @> '{"url": "https://api.stripe.com"}'
  AND te.created_at > now() - INTERVAL '7 days'
ORDER BY te.created_at DESC
LIMIT 50;

-- Add GIN index on args_json for this type of query:
-- CREATE INDEX ON tool_events USING GIN (args_json);
Real-world example

Consider a B2B agent platform that automates invoice processing: the agent reads email attachments, calls an OCR tool, validates line items, and posts to the customer's accounting API. The platform processes tens of thousands of runs per day across hundreds of tenant accounts.
The PostgreSQL schema has a
tenant_id

column on every table (row-level multi-tenancy). Runs are written by async FastAPI workers; PgBouncer sits in transaction-mode pooling, maintaining at most 50 server connections shared across 200 worker coroutines. A read replica handles the compliance export queries run nightly by the accounting team — these are long-running GROUP BY queries that would otherwise compete with write traffic on the primary.
Large payloads — the raw email bodies, PDF attachments, and full OCR JSON responses — are stored in S3. The
tool_events
table stores only the S3 object key in the
result_ref

column. This keeps average row size under 1 KB and the entire database under 50 GB despite processing millions of documents. PITR is configured with a 30-day retention window to satisfy the customer's audit requirements.

Webhook delivery idempotency is enforced by a UNIQUE constraint on webhook_deliveries(event_id). A Celery worker that crashes mid-delivery retries safely — the second INSERT raises a unique violation which the worker catches to confirm the event was already delivered and move on.
Common mistakes
Storing full LLM traces in TEXT columns

LLM conversations can be hundreds of kilobytes. Storing them as TEXT in a runs row bloats the table, inflates backup size, and slows every table scan. The correct pattern is to store the conversation in S3 and keep only the S3 object key in SQL.
Missing indexes on foreign keys and time ranges

-- WRONG: no index on run_id — becomes a full scan at scale
SELECT * FROM tool_events WHERE run_id = '...'; -- seq scan on 50M rows

-- RIGHT: create index at migration time
CREATE INDEX ON tool_events (run_id);  -- added once, used by every lookup

-- Also add partial index for active runs (avoids scanning completed runs)
CREATE INDEX ON runs (status, created_at) WHERE status IN ('queued','running');
N+1 query anti-pattern

# WRONG: one query per run to load tool events
runs = await db.execute(text("SELECT * FROM runs WHERE session_id = :s"), {"s": sid})
for run in runs:
    events = await db.execute(text("SELECT * FROM tool_events WHERE run_id = :r"), {"r": run.id})
    # N queries for N runs

# RIGHT: single join or IN query
result = await db.execute(
    text("""
        SELECT r.id, r.status, te.tool_name, te.status AS event_status
          FROM runs r
          LEFT JOIN tool_events te ON te.run_id = r.id
         WHERE r.session_id = :s
         ORDER BY r.created_at DESC, te.created_at
    """),
    {"s": sid},
)
Using sync drivers in async code

# WRONG: psycopg2 blocks the event loop, starving other coroutines
import psycopg2
conn = psycopg2.connect(DATABASE_URL)
cur = conn.cursor()
cur.execute("SELECT * FROM runs")  # blocks the entire event loop

# RIGHT: use asyncpg or SQLAlchemy async
import asyncpg
pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(DATABASE_URL)
async with pool.acquire() as conn:
    rows = await conn.fetch("SELECT * FROM runs WHERE session_id = $1", session_id)
Skipping migrations in CI

Manually applying schema changes to production without a migration tool leads to schema drift — the code expects a column that does not exist in production. Use Alembic (Python) or Flyway (JVM) and run
alembic upgrade head
as a step in the deployment pipeline before rolling out new application code.
Best practices
Use PostgreSQL with JSONB
for agent platforms that need both relational integrity and flexible semi-structured data.
Append-only tool_events —
never UPDATE or DELETE audit rows. Use a status column transition instead of deletion for soft-delete semantics.
Index every foreign key
and every column that appears in WHERE clauses with high cardinality (session_id, run_id, created_at).
Pool connections through PgBouncer
in transaction mode; set statement_cache_size=0 in asyncpg to avoid prepared statement conflicts.
Run migrations in CI/CD
as a pre-deployment step with a rollback plan (additive-only migrations are safest: add columns, never rename or drop without a two-phase deploy).
Keep large payloads in S3
and store only the reference key in SQL. Target average row size under 8 KB.
Use read replicas for analytics queries
so long-running GROUP BY queries do not contend with write traffic on the primary.
Enable logical replication
for zero-downtime major upgrades (pg_upgrade requires a maintenance window; logical replication does not).
PITR configuration —
set wal_level=replica and configure continuous archiving to S3 via pgBackRest or Barman. Test restore monthly.
Performance & scalability
Connection pooling

PostgreSQL spawns a backend process per connection. At 200 connections each using about 5 MB, the database server consumes 1 GB just for connection overhead. PgBouncer in transaction mode multiplexes many client connections onto a smaller pool of server connections, reducing memory pressure. A typical configuration allows 500 client connections mapping to 20–50 server connections.
Query planning and EXPLAIN ANALYZE

-- Always profile slow queries with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT r.id, count(te.id) AS event_count
  FROM runs r
  LEFT JOIN tool_events te ON te.run_id = r.id
 WHERE r.session_id = 'abc123'
 GROUP BY r.id;

-- Key things to look for in the output:
-- Seq Scan (bad on large tables) vs Index Scan / Bitmap Heap Scan (good)
-- Actual rows vs Estimated rows (large divergence → run ANALYZE)
-- Shared Buffers Hit vs Read (high reads → missing cache or hot data set too large)
Partitioning for time-series data
The
tool_events
table can grow to hundreds of millions of rows over time. Table partitioning by month on
created_at

allows pruning old partitions with a DROP TABLE (instantaneous) rather than a slow DELETE. Queries that filter by date automatically skip irrelevant partitions (partition pruning), dramatically reducing scan size.
Autovacuum and bloat

MVCC leaves dead row versions after updates and deletes. Autovacuum reclaims this space, but tables with very high update rates (e.g. runs.status transitions) can accumulate bloat faster than autovacuum keeps up. Monitor table bloat with the pg_stat_user_tables view and tune autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay and autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor per table if needed.
Tooling & ecosystem
Migrations

# Alembic setup (Python)
# pip install alembic sqlalchemy
alembic init alembic

# alembic/env.py — point to your models
from myapp.models import Base
target_metadata = Base.metadata

# Generate migration from model changes
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "add tool_events partition"

# Apply in deploy pipeline
alembic upgrade head

# Check current revision
alembic current
ORM — SQLAlchemy 2 async

from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, relationship
from sqlalchemy import ForeignKey, Text, Integer, TIMESTAMP
from datetime import datetime
import uuid

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Run(Base):
    __tablename__ = "runs"

    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4)
    session_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("sessions.id"))
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, default="queued")
    model: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text)
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(TIMESTAMP(timezone=True))

    tool_events: Mapped[list["ToolEvent"]] = relationship(back_populates="run")

class ToolEvent(Base):
    __tablename__ = "tool_events"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    run_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("runs.id"))
    tool_name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text)
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text)

    run: Mapped["Run"] = relationship(back_populates="tool_events")
Managed databases
AWS RDS / Aurora PostgreSQL —
automated backups, Multi-AZ failover, IAM authentication. Aurora serverless v2 scales compute to zero between workloads.
Supabase —
Postgres with PostgREST auto-API, pgvector, and auth built-in; popular for agent prototypes.
Neon —
serverless Postgres with branch-per-PR workflow; WAL shipping to S3 for PITR.
Google Cloud SQL / AlloyDB —
AlloyDB is PostgreSQL-compatible with columnar cache for analytic workloads.
Comparison with alternatives
Store
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best fit in agent platform
PostgreSQL
ACID, joins, JSONB, pgvector, mature tooling
Schema migrations, single-writer scale limit
Authoritative state: runs, sessions, billing, audit
MongoDB
Flexible schema, horizontal scale
Weaker joins, no true ACID multi-doc before 4.0
Rapidly evolving document shapes where schema is unknown
Redis
Sub-ms reads, pub/sub, atomic ops, TTL
Volatile by default, limited query model
Ephemeral queues, distributed locks, live counters
DynamoDB
Infinite scale, serverless, single-digit ms
No joins, complex data modelling, eventual consistency
High-frequency, key-lookup only access patterns at massive scale
Qdrant / Weaviate
ANN vector search, hybrid search
No relational model, no ACID
Semantic retrieval over embeddings; used alongside SQL
S3 / GCS
Cheap, unlimited, durable
No queries, high latency for small reads
Large blobs: LLM traces, uploaded files, export archives

In practice, a production agent platform uses all of these tiers together rather than picking one. SQL is the source of truth; Redis is the speed layer; object storage is the bulk layer; a vector database enables semantic search. The mistake is trying to force one store to serve all four purposes.
Security & reliability
Access control

Create a dedicated PostgreSQL role for the application with the minimum permissions it needs — typically SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE on specific tables; no DROP, no ALTER. Use IAM database authentication on AWS RDS so the application never stores a long-lived password in an environment variable. Rotate credentials with AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault.
Encryption
At rest —

enable storage encryption on the database volume (AES-256 on RDS, Transparent Data Encryption on others). Encrypt sensitive columns (e.g. api_key_hash) at the application level with pgcrypto for defense in depth.
In transit —
enforce SSL/TLS connections (sslmode=require in connection string). Reject unencrypted connections at the database server level (pg_hba.conf hostssl rule).
API key storage —
store only the bcrypt or Argon2id hash of API keys. The plaintext is shown once at creation and never stored.
SQL injection prevention

# WRONG: string interpolation — exploitable by SQL injection
query = f"SELECT * FROM runs WHERE session_id = '{session_id}'"  # NEVER do this

# RIGHT: parameterised query — database handles escaping
result = await conn.fetch(
    "SELECT * FROM runs WHERE session_id = $1",  # asyncpg uses $1 placeholders
    session_id,
)

# SQLAlchemy also uses parameterised queries by default:
await db.execute(text("SELECT * FROM runs WHERE session_id = :sid"), {"sid": session_id})
High availability
Streaming replication —
maintain at least one synchronous standby (synchronous_commit=on) to guarantee no data loss on failover.
Automated failover —
use Patroni, AWS RDS Multi-AZ, or Neon's built-in HA to promote the standby automatically within 30–60 seconds of primary failure.
Connection retry logic —
agent workers must handle transient connection errors with exponential backoff; PgBouncer absorbs brief primary restarts without dropping all client connections.
Health checks —
expose a /health endpoint that executes a lightweight SQL query (SELECT 1) so load balancers and Kubernetes liveness probes can detect database connectivity failures.
Key takeaways
SQL databases are the authoritative state layer for agentic AI: they store sessions, runs, tool events, users, and billing records with ACID guarantees that prevent partial state corruption.
PostgreSQL is the recommended default — JSONB for flexible tool arguments, pgvector for embeddings, and a mature ecosystem of async drivers, ORMs, and managed cloud offerings.
ACID transactions are non-negotiable for actions with financial or compliance impact: always bundle status updates and billing debits in a single transaction.
Large payloads (LLM traces, uploaded files) belong in object storage (S3); SQL stores only the reference key, keeping average row size small and backup costs low.
Index every foreign key, every high-cardinality filter column, and every time-range column used in dashboard queries; missing indexes are the most common production performance regression.
Use PgBouncer in transaction-mode pooling between async workers and PostgreSQL to avoid spawning hundreds of idle server processes.
Run schema migrations (Alembic, Flyway) as a pre-deployment CI/CD step; never apply DDL changes manually to production.
Defend the database with a least-privilege application role, parameterised queries (never string interpolation), SSL enforcement, and IAM-based credential rotation.
Combine SQL (structured state), Redis (ephemeral speed layer), object storage (bulk), and a vector database (semantic search) — each tier serves a distinct purpose and none replaces the others.
